text: One common issue with personalization in all LLMs is how distracting memory seems to be for the models. A single question from 2 months ago about some topic can keep coming up as some kind of a deep interest of mine with undue mentions in perpetuity. Some kind of trying too hard.

(I cycle through all LLMs over time and all of them seem to do this so it's not any particular implementation but something deeper, e.g. maybe during training, a lot of the information in the context window is relevant to the task, so the LLMs develop a bias to use what is given,

Enterprises have attempted to mass-adopt AI and have almost nothing to show for it.

They bought the AI-native CRM, they tried n8n, and they even let a large consulting firm run a 6-month "AI strategy" engagement that produced a PDF & a pilot that never landed.

The unfortunate https://t.co/XUDRVdgQuV

There are 3 core philosophies that we believe in when it comes to implementing AI for a large company: 

[1]. Build on top of the existing systems of record. 

Your company spent years and millions of dollars building its stack. NetSuite, Salesforce, SAP, ServiceNow, whatever it

Every business runs differently. We know this, which is why before we attempt to build anything, we audit. 

Skipping this step is why 95% of AI pilots fail, and the average enterprise company has seen precisely 0 ROI from AI adoption; they jumped to building before they

CFOs: 

Your finance team's month-end close takes 18-22 days because your controller is manually checking 14 separate Excel files across entities. 70% of your AP exceptions are the same 12 types recurring every single month. 

Your FP&A team can't deliver variance analysis until

CROs: 

Your reps spend 38% of their time on internal work. Updating Salesforce, chasing deal desk approvals, routing contracts through legal, reconciling comp disputes. 

Your deals aren't dying because the buyer said no, they're dying because internal process took so long that

COOs:

Your ops team is the integration layer between your systems. When a disruption hits, someone has to check SAP for inventory, Manhattan for warehouse allocation, Blue Yonder for demand forecast, then call 3-4 people to coordinate a response. Average response time: 2-4 days.

When we deploy, your team works alongside the agents. Day one, they approve every action. Week two, they're only reviewing exceptions. By go-live, agents are running at 90%+ accuracy on production work and your people are managing output instead of doing it themselves.

We deploy

And finally: we're hiring.

We're building out our team in San Francisco and looking for engineers and strategists who want to deploy AI agents in production at companies doing billions in revenue. 

Unlimited token budgets. We encourage you to automate your entire job, because

🚨 Karpathy just described every AI memory feature ever built.

One question from 2 months ago = Claude mentioning it in every response forever.

The fix takes 3 minutes:
Settings → Capabilities → Memory → "View and edit your memory"
